answer: My full name is Putra Christian.
I'd describe myself as a creative front-end developer building playful, polished, people-friendly web experiences.
I'm a front-end developer with 5+ years of experience building responsive websites, interactive interfaces, and digital experiences that mix personality with clarity.

I like frontend that feels alive. Sometimes that means bold layout, sometimes tiny interaction details, and sometimes just making something clearer, faster, and more enjoyable to use. My background includes years of shipping work in a fast-moving environment at Garena, but I don't want to be boxed into one niche. What I bring more broadly is creative UI thinking, responsive implementation, interface polish, and the ability to turn visual ideas into real web experiences people want to explore.
I'm based in Jakarta, Indonesia.
I'm open to freelance, contract, and creative collaboration opportunities.
You can reach me at putra3christian@gmail.com.
You can find me by email, on LinkedIn at linkedin.com/in/putra-christian, on my site at putrachristian.github.io, and my resume is downloadable from the site.
I've been doing frontend for more than 5 years.
Right now my site highlights 5+ years building for the web, 6+ projects in the library, and 3 talks or workshops.
My specialty is creative frontend work — responsive interfaces, interactive websites, polished UI, and digital experiences that feel lively, clear, and fun to use.
My style leans playful, polished, and people-friendly. I want interfaces to feel memorable and alive while still being readable, responsive, and easy to use.

Yeah, for sure. A lot of my professional background comes from gaming, but my frontend skills fit product sites, landing pages, branded microsites, interactive campaigns, and plenty of other digital experiences outside gaming.
I like working closely with designers, backend devs, and product folks to turn concepts, mockups, and wireframes into production-ready frontend.
Performance is a big part of how I work — keeping interfaces fast, responsive, and stable even when they're visually rich.
My core stack is JavaScript (ES6+), HTML5, CSS3, React.js, Redux, Node.js, Next.js, Sass, Styled-Components, Git, and modern package managers like npm, yarn, and bun.
On the interface side, I do responsive web design, single-page apps, visual hierarchy, interaction design thinking, cross-browser work, and I'm comfortable integrating with RESTful APIs.
My production habits lean on clean code, component thinking, UI polish, Git-based workflows, performance awareness, and maintainable frontend.
I usually start by getting a feel for the energy and visual direction a UI should carry before I get into implementation details.
I like interfaces with personality, but only when they still feel obvious and easy to move through.
The final build has to be responsive, maintainable, and smooth enough to survive real use — not just look good in a screenshot.
I currently work at PT. Garena Indonesia as an Associate Front End Web Developer — I've been in this role since February 2019.

At Garena I build interactive web experiences, frontend features, and polished UI in a fast-moving production environment. Day-to-day that's React.js, Redux, JavaScript (ES6+), performance tuning, responsive implementation, and turning concepts into production-ready UI.

Some highlights from my time at Garena: building interfaces with React.js and Redux, collaborating across teams, optimizing loading speed and performance, and sticking to clean code plus cross-device best practices.

Before my full-time role, I interned as a Front End Web Developer at PT. Garena Indonesia from August 2018 to January 2019. That period was mostly testing, debugging, UI component support, and static page implementation with HTML and CSS.

topic: Create Your Own Personal Page with React JS
answer: In March 2021, I spoke at a public webinar called 'Create Your Own Personal Page with React JS' with AMBIZ and Himpunan Mahasiswa Informatika ITB.
In June 2021, I spoke at 'Belajar bikin game bareng Garena' with Skilvul, sharing practical frontend web dev ideas in a collaborative workshop.

topic: How to Develop a Web Game
tags: how to develop a web game
answer: In February 2024, I joined Skilvul as a guest speaker for 'How to Develop a Web Game', walking through practical frontend thinking for interactive web experiences.
I built this site like a growing project library instead of a one-shot static showcase, so I can keep adding new work without redesigning the whole thing every time.

topic: Why many projects have no live demo
answer: A lot of my projects don't have live demos because they come from private or time-limited commercial work, so I use screenshots and public-safe summaries to represent them responsibly.

Interactive Launch Experience is one of my featured landing-page style projects — bold layout, layered storytelling, strong first impressions, smooth responsive interaction. My role there was frontend implementation, responsive UI, and visual polish.

Content-Rich Microsite is a featured microsite-style project built around atmosphere, pacing, and content balance. It's a good example of how I shape a lot of content into something still enjoyable to browse with modular sections and responsive adaptation.

Reward And Progress Flow is a featured product-flow project about clarity, repeated use, and satisfying interaction. It's how I think about dense content, repeat visits, and making functional flows feel lighter and more fun.

Premium Feature Landing is the kind of work where multiple tiers, features, or value points need to come across clearly without losing visual appeal. My contribution focused on frontend implementation and content structuring.

Interactive Prediction Flow is about organizing longer content flows so they stay inviting, understandable, and interesting to browse. It shows how I handle pacing, readability, and interface guidance.
Mobile-First Action Page is a utility-style project focused on fast understanding and clear action. It's how I keep mobile-first work effective, visually clean, and low-friction.

topic: Kinds of projects in the portfolio
answer: My project library covers landing pages, microsites, product-style flows, interaction-heavy experiences, and mobile-first utility pages. Across all of them the work emphasizes responsive UI, visual storytelling, clarity, and frontend polish.

topic: Why the portfolio is structured as a library
answer: I set the site up as a library because the goal is long-term growth — I can drop in new projects continuously without rebuilding the whole portfolio each time.

topic: Whether I only fit gaming projects
answer: Nope, not at all. I'm intentionally positioning myself more broadly. The same frontend strengths fit product sites, branded campaigns, landing pages, interactive websites, and plenty of other digital experiences.
This chat is set up for questions about what frontend work I enjoy most, how my experience fits outside gaming, the tech I use most often, and how I'd describe my style.
